title: The Dot Product
kicker: How much do two directions agree?

chapters.narration: Okay — picture this. You and I are both out hiking, but we set off in different directions. Here's the question I love: how much do our two paths actually agree? Are we heading roughly the same way, wandering off at right angles, or marching in total opposite directions? There's one neat little tool that answers that, exactly. It's called the dot product. Think of it as a friendship meter for directions.

So here's the whole idea in one breath. The dot product takes two vectors and hands you a single number for how much they point the same way. Positive means we're basically headed together. Zero means we're at right angles — total strangers, mathematically speaking. And negative means we're pulling in opposite directions. Positive, zero, negative. That's the agreement scale, and once you feel it, you'll start seeing it everywhere.
chapters.formula: u · v  >  0   →   same way        =  0   →   perpendicular        <  0   →   opposite

chapters.eyebrow: Chapter 3 · Two ways to see it
chapters.narration: Now, there are two ways to actually get that number — and the magic is they always agree. The first is pure arithmetic: multiply the matching pieces and add them up. u-one times v-one, plus u-two times v-two. The second is geometric: take how long each vector is, and multiply by the cosine of the angle between them. One way is counting; the other is measuring a shadow. They land on the exact same answer. That's not a coincidence — that's the whole beautiful point.
chapters.formula: u · v  =  u₁v₁ + u₂v₂  =  |u| |v| cos θ

chapters.narration: Alright — this is where you stop listening to me and start feeling it. Grab the two arrows and drag them around. Watch the meter. Swing them together and it glows positive. Pull them to a right angle and it drops to zero. Push past that, into opposite corners, and it goes negative. Try to land each one. This is the dot product, live, in your hands.

check.question: Two hiking paths are the vectors [3, 4] and [1, 2]. What's their dot product — and what does it mean?
options: 11 — the paths head in generally the same direction
-1 — the paths point in opposite directions
0 — there's no relationship between the paths
check.explanation: [3,4] · [1,2] = (3)(1) + (4)(2) = 3 + 8 = 11. It's positive, so the two paths point generally the same way — those hikers are roughly headed together.

chapters.narration: Here's why I get excited about this one. Every time Google decides a web page matches your search — that's a dot product. Every time Spotify lines up a song with your taste — dot product. Machine learning runs billions of them a second, all asking the same simple question: how much do these two things agree? So remember the scale. Positive is together, zero is strangers, negative is opposite. Direction and strength — that's my whole personality, and now it's a little bit yours too.
